coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria completeness and provability with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and provability

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required commit subject format (e.g. `Release 0.10.1`) to Plan Step 5 so that the AC3 proving command `git log origin/main --oneline | grep -i "release 0.10.1"` reliably matches the release commit.
evidence: Step 5: 'Commit with rebar-ticket: zincoid-panoramic-eeve + DCO sign-off trailers' — subject format not specified
AC3 proving command: 'git log origin/main --oneline | grep -i "release 0.10.1"'
ABSENCE: no required commit subject format stated in the plan
findings.finding: AC3's proving command (`git log origin/main --oneline | grep -i "release 0.10.1"`) assumes the commit subject line contains the phrase 'release 0.10.1', but Plan Step 5 only mandates specific trailers (rebar-ticket + DCO sign-off) and says nothing about the required commit subject format. If the executor writes a subject like 'Bump version to 0.10.1' or 'v0.10.1', the grep silently returns no output even though the commit landed successfully.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 proving command; Plan Step 5 (commit instructions)
scenarios: Executor writes subject 'Bump rebar to v0.10.1'; commit lands on main; AC3 grep returns empty; operator incorrectly diagnoses a failed merge.
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 5, specify the commit subject explicitly: `git commit -s -m 'Release 0.10.1' --trailer 'rebar-ticket: zincoid-panoramic-eeve'`. This guarantees the AC3 grep will match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or operator-attested note) confirming that `python -m build && pipx run twine check dist/*` passed locally before the release commit was pushed, so the described artifact metadata gate has a covering criterion.
evidence: "build+validate the artifact metadata exactly as the runbook (docs/releasing.md L259) and release.yml specify: 'python -m build && python -m twine check dist/*'"
"twine is not a dev extra in pyproject; invoke it as the CI lock does via 'pipx run twine check dist/*' (or 'uvx twine check dist/*') if 'python -m twine' is absent"
No AC line references twine check, dist/*, or artifact metadata validation.
findings.finding: Step 4 describes a specific, carefully-specified local gate — 'python -m build && python -m twine check dist/*' (with noted pipx/uvx fallback) — as a meaningful pre-push validation step, but no AC verifies that this artifact metadata check was performed or passed. The Gerrit Verified +1 (AC3) covers CI gates but not the local pre-push twine check the plan explicitly emphasizes.
findings.location: Plan Step 4 / Acceptance Criteria (no matching AC)
scenarios: Operator skips the local twine check (e.g. forgets the pipx invocation); a malformed wheel is pushed to Gerrit and CI catches it only if CI also runs twine — but if CI does not replicate this check, a defective artifact reaches PyPI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: `- [ ] [operator-attested] Artifact metadata check passed: \'python -m build && pipx run twine check dist/*\' (or uvx equivalent) ran cleanly against the built wheel+sdist on the release commit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the AC1 pyproject.toml grep pattern to avoid substring false-positives, e.g. `grep -cP '(?<![0-9])0\.10\.1(?![0-9])' pyproject.toml` or `grep -c '"0.10.1"' pyproject.toml`, or note that the standalone grep is illustrative and `release_guards.py version-lockstep` is the authoritative check.
evidence: Taxonomy item (1): unanchored grep pattern — `0.10.1` matches `0.10.10`
The plan itself notes the real gate is `release_guards.py version-lockstep`
findings.finding: The AC1 proving command `grep -c 0.10.1 pyproject.toml` uses an unanchored substring pattern: it will also match `0.10.10`, `0.10.11`, etc. in future releases, and the threshold `>=1` is loose enough to pass on any spurious match. While the real enforcement gate is `release_guards.py version-lockstep`, the AC's standalone proving command is misleading as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 proving command for pyproject.toml
scenarios: In a future 0.10.10 release, the AC1 command is copy-pasted and the pattern `0.10.1` matches `0.10.10` lines, producing a spurious pass and masking a missing version bump.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: `grep -c '"0.10.1"' pyproject.toml` (matches the quoted TOML value) and `grep -c '"0.10.1"' server.json` == 2, which are anchored to the value context and won't match `0.10.10`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
